source_paper_title: Online Regret Bounds for Satisficing in Markov Decision Processes

Note that here our theoretical results may be suboptimal in the worst-case as we cannot achieve \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\) worst-case expected regret. Further study in this direction is a valuable future work.
title: Achieve square-root worst-case expected regret for bandit optimistic Adversarial MDPs
background_latex:
Consider an episodic loop-free adversarial Markov decision process (AMDP) with a finite state space \(\mathcal X\), finite action space \(\mathcal A\), and a fixed horizon (number of layers) \(L\). Episodes are indexed by \(t=1,\dots,T\). In episode \(t\), an adversary selects a cost function \(c_t:\mathcal X\times\mathcal A\to[0,1]\). The transition kernel \(P(\cdot\mid x,a)\) is stationary, and the state space is layered \(\mathcal X=\bigsqcup_{\ell=0}^L \mathcal X_\ell\) with transitions only from \(\mathcal X_\ell\) to \(\mathcal X_{\ell+1}\). An (occupancy-measure) policy in episode \(t\) is represented by an occupancy measure \(\rho_t\in\Delta(\mathcal M)\), where \(\Delta(\mathcal M)\) denotes the set of feasible occupancy measures induced by stationary randomized policies under \(P\). The expected episode cost of \(\rho\) under cost \(c\) is the linear functional \(\langle \rho,c\rangle = \sum_{x\in\mathcal X}\sum_{a\in\mathcal A} \rho(x,a)c(x,a).\)

A learning algorithm outputs \(\rho_t\) each episode and is evaluated by (static) regret against the best fixed comparator occupancy measure \(\rho^*\in\Delta(\mathcal M)\):
\[
R_T(\rho^*,\{c_t\}_{t=1}^T)=\sum_{t=1}^T \langle \rho_t-\rho^*,c_t\rangle.
\]
In the bandit-feedback setting, in episode \(t\) the learner observes only the realized costs along its trajectory (the costs \(c_t(x_\ell,a_\ell)\) for visited state-action pairs), not the full cost function \(c_t\).

The learner is also given a sequence of cost predictors \(M_t:\mathcal X\times\mathcal A\to[0,1]\). Define the per-episode prediction error \(\sigma_t = c_t - M_t\). The paper studies algorithms whose regret bounds are optimistic in that they depend on the cumulative prediction errors \(\{\sigma_t\}\) and can be much smaller than worst-case bounds when the predictors are accurate.
problem_definition_latex:
Design and analyze a bandit-feedback online learning algorithm for AMDPs with predictors \(\{M_t\}_{t=1}^T\) whose expected regret satisfies a worst-case bound of order \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\) (up to problem-dependent polylogarithmic factors), while still remaining optimistic in that the bound degrades gracefully with the cumulative prediction error sequence \(\{\sigma_t\}_{t=1}^T\) (for example, yielding constant or substantially sub-\(\sqrt{T}\) regret when \(\sum_{t=1}^T\|\sigma_t\|\) is small).

Equivalently: close the gap between the paper's bandit expected regret \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) worst-case rate and the \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\) rate known in related non-optimistic or different-feedback AMDP/bandit settings, without losing the optimistic dependence on \(\{\sigma_t\}\).
latex:
Consider an episodic loop-free adversarial Markov decision process (AMDP) with a finite state space \(\mathcal X\), finite action space \(\mathcal A\), and a fixed horizon (number of layers) \(L\). Episodes are indexed by \(t=1,\dots,T\). In episode \(t\), an adversary selects a cost function \(c_t:\mathcal X\times\mathcal A\to[0,1]\). The transition kernel \(P(\cdot\mid x,a)\) is stationary, and the state space is layered \(\mathcal X=\bigsqcup_{\ell=0}^L \mathcal X_\ell\) with transitions only from \(\mathcal X_\ell\) to \(\mathcal X_{\ell+1}\). An (occupancy-measure) policy in episode \(t\) is represented by an occupancy measure \(\rho_t\in\Delta(\mathcal M)\), where \(\Delta(\mathcal M)\) denotes the set of feasible occupancy measures induced by stationary randomized policies under \(P\). The expected episode cost of \(\rho\) under cost \(c\) is the linear functional \(\langle \rho,c\rangle = \sum_{x\in\mathcal X}\sum_{a\in\mathcal A} \rho(x,a)c(x,a).\)

A learning algorithm outputs \(\rho_t\) each episode and is evaluated by (static) regret against the best fixed comparator occupancy measure \(\rho^*\in\Delta(\mathcal M)\):
\[
R_T(\rho^*,\{c_t\}_{t=1}^T)=\sum_{t=1}^T \langle \rho_t-\rho^*,c_t\rangle.
\]
In the bandit-feedback setting, in episode \(t\) the learner observes only the realized costs along its trajectory (the costs \(c_t(x_\ell,a_\ell)\) for visited state-action pairs), not the full cost function \(c_t\).

The learner is also given a sequence of cost predictors \(M_t:\mathcal X\times\mathcal A\to[0,1]\). Define the per-episode prediction error \(\sigma_t = c_t - M_t\). The paper studies algorithms whose regret bounds are optimistic in that they depend on the cumulative prediction errors \(\{\sigma_t\}\) and can be much smaller than worst-case bounds when the predictors are accurate.

Design and analyze a bandit-feedback online learning algorithm for AMDPs with predictors \(\{M_t\}_{t=1}^T\) whose expected regret satisfies a worst-case bound of order \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\) (up to problem-dependent polylogarithmic factors), while still remaining optimistic in that the bound degrades gracefully with the cumulative prediction error sequence \(\{\sigma_t\}_{t=1}^T\) (for example, yielding constant or substantially sub-\(\sqrt{T}\) regret when \(\sum_{t=1}^T\|\sigma_t\|\) is small).

Equivalently: close the gap between the paper's bandit expected regret \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) worst-case rate and the \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\) rate known in related non-optimistic or different-feedback AMDP/bandit settings, without losing the optimistic dependence on \(\{\sigma_t\}\).
location_in_paper: Page 7, Section 4 (discussion immediately after Theorem 2: Bandit – Expected).
open_problem_quote: Note that here our theoretical results may be suboptimal in the worst-case as we cannot achieve \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\) worst-case expected regret. Further study in this direction is a valuable future work.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is existential (seek an improved algorithm and proof) rather than a universal inequality that can be falsified by a finite counterexample search. Automated instance generation can test candidate algorithms empirically, but cannot certify impossibility of \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\) worst-case expected regret in this setting.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize and code families of bandit AMDP algorithms (estimators, exploration schemes, step sizes) and use simulators to approximate worst-case/robust regret over adversarially generated cost sequences as a fitness signal. However, the true objective is a worst-case theoretical guarantee, and empirical fitness is only a proxy; moreover, searching over adversarial sequences and AMDP instances is computationally heavy and may not correlate perfectly with provable \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\) rates.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks for a bandit-feedback algorithm for episodic loop-free adversarial MDPs that is simultaneously (i) optimistic with respect to a predictor sequence \(M_t\) (so regret scales with cumulative prediction error \(\sigma_t=c_t-M_t\)) and (ii) achieves \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) worst-case expected regret. The source paper (Moon & Hashemi, 2024) makes clear why this is nontrivial in the AMDP trajectory-feedback model: standard importance-weighted or implicit-exploration estimators (as used in OREPS and later AMDP bandit work) do not yield variance terms that can be controlled by \(|c_t-M_t|\), so optimism and bandit variance control conflict. Their proposed estimator \(\hat c_t(x,a)=\frac{c_t(x,a)-M_t(x,a)}{\rho_t(x,a)+\gamma}\mathbf 1\{(x,a)\text{ visited}\}+M_t(x,a)\) restores an optimistic variance proxy, leading to optimistic bounds but with worst-case expected regret \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) and high-probability \(\tilde O(T^{3/4})\), leaving a gap to \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\).

Prior non-optimistic AMDP bandit literature demonstrates that \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\)-type worst-case rates are achievable when one does not insist on predictor-dependent optimism: OREPS (Zimin & Neu, 2013) and subsequent analyses of online MDPs under bandit feedback (Neu et al., 2014) provide the occupancy-measure mirror-descent template and bandit estimators, while Jin et al. (ICML 2020) show strong \(\sqrt T\)-scaling even with unknown transitions via implicit exploration and confidence sets. On the optimistic side, Rakhlin & Sridharan (COLT 2013) provide the canonical predictable-sequence framework (optimistic mirror descent) that achieves \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) worst-case and smaller regret when predictions are accurate, but their bandit model differs from AMDP trajectory feedback. Bridging these lines likely requires new estimator/regularizer combinations that preserve the predictor-controlled variance property of Moon & Hashemi while matching the \(\sqrt T\) worst-case scaling of implicit-exploration/log-barrier approaches.

Promising directions suggested by the surrounding literature include: (i) combining optimistic baselines with log-barrier or other self-concordant regularizers that yield data-dependent \(\sqrt T\) bounds in adversarial bandits/MDPs (Lee et al., NeurIPS 2020), (ii) designing hybrid estimators that interpolate between predictor-based control variates and implicit exploration so that the variance term scales like \(\min\{1/(\rho_t+\gamma),\,1/(\rho_t+\gamma)^2\}\) in a way compatible with \(\sqrt T\) optimization, and (iii) leveraging non-stationary RL techniques (Fei et al., 2020; Wei & Luo, 2021) to relate predictor error to variation measures while retaining static regret guarantees. As of the provided materials, no paper resolves the \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) to \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) worst-case expected regret gap under bandit trajectory feedback while keeping optimistic dependence on \(\{\sigma_t\}\); thus the problem remains open.

sources.title: Online learning in episodic Markovian decision processes by relative entropy policy search
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces OREPS, an occupancy-measure mirror-descent/FTRL method with relative-entropy regularization for episodic loop-free AMDPs, yielding sublinear regret under adversarial costs (full information and bandit variants via importance weighting).
sources.connection: Provides the baseline AMDP policy-search framework (occupancy measures, KL regularization, exponential updates) that optimistic bandit algorithms such as OREPS-OPIX build on; also highlights the variance issues of naive importance-weighted estimators that the open problem seeks to overcome while achieving \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) worst-case expected regret.

sources.title: Online Markov decision processes under bandit feedback
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies online MDPs with adversarial costs under bandit feedback and develops algorithms with sublinear regret, establishing foundational techniques for bandit feedback in AMDPs.
sources.connection: Represents the non-optimistic bandit-feedback AMDP literature where \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\)-type rates are achievable under certain models; serves as a benchmark target rate that the optimistic-with-predictors setting has not yet matched in worst-case expected regret.

sources.title: Online learning with predictable sequences
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops optimistic online learning (e.g., optimistic mirror descent) with regret scaling with cumulative prediction error \(\sum_t \|c_t-M_t\|_*^2\), yielding \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) worst-case and much smaller regret for accurate predictors.
sources.connection: Provides the canonical optimistic regret template the open problem aims to extend to AMDPs under bandit trajectory feedback, while retaining worst-case \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) expected regret.

sources.title: Learning adversarial Markov decision processes with bandit feedback and unknown transition
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives near-optimal regret guarantees for adversarial MDPs with bandit feedback and unknown transitions using implicit exploration and confidence sets, achieving \(\tilde O(L|\mathcal X|\sqrt{|\mathcal A|T})\)-type bounds (up to factors) in the non-optimistic setting.
sources.connection: Shows that \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) worst-case rates are achievable in adversarial MDPs with bandit feedback (even with unknown transitions) without predictors; the open problem is to incorporate predictors optimistically without worsening worst-case expected regret.

sources.title: Bias no more: high-probability data-dependent regret bounds for adversarial bandits and mdps
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces log-barrier/biased estimators yielding high-probability, data-dependent regret bounds for adversarial bandits and MDPs, including \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\)-type guarantees with dependence on the comparator loss.
sources.connection: Provides alternative variance-control and high-probability analysis tools (log-barrier, data-dependent bounds) that may be combined with optimistic predictors to aim for \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) worst-case expected regret while keeping optimism.

sources.title: Non-stationary reinforcement learning without prior knowledge: an optimal black-box approach
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops optimal black-box methods for non-stationary RL with dynamic regret bounds such as \(\tilde O(\min\{\sqrt{QT},\Delta^{1/3}T^{2/3}\})\) under bandit feedback.
sources.connection: While focused on non-stationarity measures (changes \(Q,\Delta\)) rather than predictors \(M_t\), it offers techniques for handling time-varying costs and may inform optimistic predictor-based designs aiming to recover \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) worst-case expected regret.

sources.title: Dynamic regret of policy optimization in non-stationary environments
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes dynamic regret for policy optimization methods in non-stationary environments, including \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\)-type dependencies under suitable variation measures.
sources.connection: Represents a line of work that attains \(\sqrt T\)-style rates for changing costs by controlling variation, suggesting that combining predictor accuracy measures with policy optimization may help close the \(T^{2/3}\) worst-case gap in optimistic bandit AMDPs.

sources.title: No-regret learning with high-probability in adversarial markov decision processes
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides high-probability no-regret guarantees for adversarial MDPs using implicit exploration estimators and refined concentration arguments.
sources.connection: Shows that high-probability control of bandit estimators in AMDPs is possible without predictors; relevant as a baseline and as a source of techniques to potentially integrate predictors while targeting \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) worst-case expected regret.
